_comment: Rotating content bank. The script picks items deterministically by date so each day's edition is different. Edit freely — just keep the shape.

newsbrief.lead: Brits now spend an average of 14 hours a week on streaming — more than cooking, cleaning and exercising combined.
newsbrief.detail: The biggest jump is in the over-55s, who used to be the holdouts for live telly. 'Second-screening' means we pay less attention than ever.

newsbrief.lead: 62% of us have lied about reading a book to look smart in front of someone we fancy.
newsbrief.detail: Most-faked titles: 1984, Pride and Prejudice and (awkwardly) the Bible. One in five admit to faking a whole dinner-party conversation about a novel they'd only seen the cover of.

newsbrief.lead: Holiday firms report a 40% spike in 'shoulder season' bookings — September and October sun trips are way up on last year.
newsbrief.detail: Travellers blame school-holiday flight prices, unreliable summer weather, and 'August feels too crowded these days'. Spain, Greece and Portugal top the wish list.

newsbrief.lead: More than half of UK workers would take a 10% pay cut to never go into the office again — but only 17% of bosses say they'd offer one.
newsbrief.detail: The gap is growing again after forced return-to-office rules. HR experts say the tension shows up in resignation rates, particularly for under-35s.

newsbrief.lead: Three quarters of dog owners now talk to their dog more than they talk to their partner.
newsbrief.detail: Top topics: 'what we're doing later', 'what we just saw out the window' and a surprising amount of Bake Off commentary. 12% admit they've apologised to the dog after a row with the human.

newsbrief.lead: Britain's most-stolen supermarket item in 2026 isn't meat or cheese — it's olive oil, with theft up 220% year on year.
newsbrief.detail: Prices have nearly doubled in two years. Some shops have fitted security tags to single bottles, others have moved premium brands behind the counter — like cigarettes.

newsbrief.lead: The fastest-growing hobby of 2026 is hand-embroidery — sales of needles and thread up 180% in a year.
newsbrief.detail: Crafters say it's the perfect 'phone detox' — slow, tactile, satisfying. TikTok is full of 20-somethings showing off custom denim jackets.

newsbrief.lead: Almost two thirds of us say we've cried at a podcast in the last year — grief, parenting and identity top the list of triggers.
newsbrief.detail: Researchers reckon it's the intimacy of audio that does it. The figure is even higher for under-30s, 71% of whom admit a recent podcast-induced tear.

newsbrief.lead: Half of UK adults can't name a single member of the cabinet — but 80% can name three Love Island contestants.
newsbrief.detail: Political engagement researchers say it's not apathy so much as 'noise overload'. People remember faces from things they actively watch.

newsbrief.lead: The most-Googled DIY question in Britain this year: 'how to remove silicone'.
newsbrief.detail: Tradespeople say it's the rise of botched bathroom resealing jobs during lockdown that's still haunting people. YouTube tutorials on the topic have racked up 60 million views.

newsbrief.lead: One in three of us has had a 'sleep divorce' — sleeping in a separate bed from our partner.
newsbrief.detail: Reasons: snoring (45%), different schedules (30%), restless legs, and one bizarre 12% who say their partner 'talks to the cat in their sleep'.

newsbrief.lead: British baby names are getting shorter again — three- and four-letter names are dominating the registrar.
newsbrief.detail: Top picks this year: Noa, Ezra, Ada and Bo. Long, multi-syllable Victorian names like Theodora are sliding back down the chart.

newsbrief.lead: The average Brit checks their phone 159 times a day — but says it 'doesn't feel like that much'.
newsbrief.detail: Researchers using passive monitoring apps say we badly underestimate our own usage. Most people guess between 30 and 70 times.

newsbrief.lead: Cash is making a small comeback — ATM withdrawals are up 14% on this time last year.
newsbrief.detail: Bank analysts credit budget-conscious households using cash to stay disciplined. The 'envelope method' is back trending on TikTok.

newsbrief.lead: Roses are out, peonies are in — UK florists report a 200% rise in peony orders for weddings this summer.
newsbrief.detail: Brides cite 'softer, less staged' aesthetics. Wholesale peony prices have crept up accordingly.

newsbrief.lead: Pubs report a Tuesday boom — the new 'mid-week meet' is the fastest-growing dining occasion.
newsbrief.detail: Operators say WFH-ers like getting out the house mid-week without the weekend crowds. Tuesday wine sales are up almost 30%.

newsbrief.lead: Loneliness is now considered a bigger health risk than smoking 15 cigarettes a day, according to NHS England.
newsbrief.detail: Social prescribing — GPs referring patients to community activities — is rolling out nationwide. Walking groups, book clubs and choirs are reportedly oversubscribed.

newsbrief.lead: Almost 70% of UK households now have a smart speaker — but the most common use is still 'asking what time it is'.
newsbrief.detail: Researchers say we under-use the devices we own. Setting timers, playing music and weather queries make up two thirds of all commands.

newsbrief.lead: The 'silver gamer' is the fastest-growing demographic in UK gaming — over-65s spent £180 million on games last year.
newsbrief.detail: Cosy life-sim titles like Stardew Valley and Animal Crossing dominate. Console makers are reportedly designing larger UI for the market.

newsbrief.lead: Brits spend an average of 38 minutes a day deciding what to watch — and 12 of those are spent scrolling Netflix.
newsbrief.detail: 'Watch-paralysis' has its own subreddit. Streamers are quietly experimenting with 'shuffle' buttons that just play something.

survey.question: 71% of us admit to doing THIS in the car when we think no-one is watching. What is it?
survey.answer: singing at the top of our lungs, with full dramatic facial expressions.

survey.question: Asked 'what's the most British thing about your morning?', the number one answer wasn't a cup of tea — it was THIS.
survey.answer: apologising to someone who walked into you.

survey.question: 48% of us do THIS every single day. What is it?
63% of us have done THIS in a meeting we're meant to be paying attention to. What?
82% of us secretly enjoy doing THIS, even though we'd never admit it. What?
When asked what they'd grab in a house fire after their pets and family, 40% said THIS. What?
57% of UK workers admit doing THIS while on a work call. What?
Asked what makes them feel old, the top answer wasn't grey hairs — it was THIS. What?
survey.answer: not recognising any of the names at the top of the charts.

survey.question: 44% of us have done THIS while waiting for a kettle to boil. What?
survey.answer: wandered off and forgotten about it entirely.

survey.question: 75% of us check THIS more than ten times a day. What?
61% of us have lied about THIS to get out of a social event. What?
53% of people admit to doing THIS in a restaurant when no-one's looking. What?
69% of Brits do THIS as soon as they get in from work. What?
survey.answer: take their trousers off and put pyjama bottoms on.

survey.question: 39% of us have done THIS in a supermarket trolley aisle. What?

talk_topic.lead: A new study says we form a strong opinion of someone within 7 seconds of meeting them.
talk_topic.detail: Talk-back: what's the quickest you've ever decided you didn't like someone — and were you right? Or the quickest you DID like someone?

talk_topic.lead: Matching pet-and-owner outfits are apparently making a comeback this summer.
talk_topic.detail: Would you actually do it? And if so — does the dog get a vote in the wardrobe?

talk_topic.lead: If you could only listen to one song for the rest of your life, what is it?
talk_topic.detail: Talk-back: pick one. No 'depends on the mood'. Just one song forever — go.

talk_topic.lead: What's the most British thing you've ever done?
talk_topic.detail: Talk-back: bonus points for the apology, the queue and the cup of tea all in one anecdote.

talk_topic.lead: A survey says 30% of us have a 'forever favourite' meal we'd be happy eating every day.
talk_topic.detail: Talk-back: what's yours? And could you really eat it every day?

talk_topic.lead: Researchers say the average person has 27 unread voicemails. We're voicemail-phobic.
talk_topic.detail: Talk-back: when did you last actually listen to one? And who left it?

talk_topic.lead: If you won the lottery tonight, what's the FIRST thing you'd buy tomorrow?
talk_topic.detail: Talk-back: not the dream house. Not the holiday. The first daft thing you'd buy because you finally could.

talk_topic.lead: What's the worst job you've ever had — and how long did you last?
talk_topic.detail: Talk-back: the war stories are usually the funniest. Bonus marks for under a fortnight.

talk_topic.lead: A study claims our taste in music is 'locked in' by age 14.
talk_topic.detail: Talk-back: do you still listen to what you loved at 14? And what was on your bedroom wall?

talk_topic.lead: What's the most embarrassing thing in your kitchen that you'd hide if guests came round?
talk_topic.detail: Talk-back: bonus marks for anyone admitting to a Slap Chop or a NutriBullet bought once and never used.

talk_topic.lead: Apparently 1 in 5 of us have a 'comfort show' we've watched more than 10 times.
talk_topic.detail: Talk-back: what's yours? Friends? Gavin and Stacey? Bake Off? Reveal yourself.

talk_topic.lead: If you could un-invent ONE thing from modern life, what would it be?
talk_topic.detail: Talk-back: not phones. Pick something specific. Self-checkouts? Wireless earbuds that always die? The Selfridges Christmas advert?

talk_topic.lead: Researchers say the most stressful day to drive in the UK isn't Monday — it's THIS day.
talk_topic.detail: (Spoiler: it's Thursday.) Talk-back: what day of the week feels longest to you, and why?

talk_topic.lead: The 'big shop' is dying — younger Brits are doing 4–6 small shops a week instead.
talk_topic.detail: Talk-back: are you a one-big-shop person or a daily-corner-shop person? And which one is your partner?

true_false.fact: A council in the North-West has agreed to fix a much-complained-about pothole only after a resident planted a small tree in it and the photo went viral. The pothole had been reported 17 times in a year before the tree solved it overnight.
true_false.fiction: A Devon village has voted unanimously to ban Vinted couriers from parking on its narrow high street, after a single delivery driver knocked over the same pub's hanging basket three weeks in a row.

true_false.fact: A Yorkshire farmer has won a planning row by getting his prize-winning sheep listed as 'agricultural heritage', preventing a housing development from going ahead next to his field.
true_false.fiction: A Cornish café has started charging customers extra if they ask for the WiFi password before placing their order, claiming it 'improves table turnover by 40%'.

true_false.fact: A goldfish at Bristol Aquarium has been confirmed as the oldest in captivity in the UK after celebrating its 23rd birthday last week. Staff say its secret is a 'strict diet and very calm music'.
true_false.fiction: A Scottish brewery has begun training rescue dogs to detect over-fermented kegs by scent, with the lead beagle reportedly never wrong in six months.

true_false.fact: A start-up has invented a wearable that gently vibrates to remind you to drink water — and reportedly raised £3 million in two days on Kickstarter.
true_false.fiction: Researchers at Manchester University have built an AI that can predict which biscuit you'll dunk in your tea next, based only on the way you hold the mug.

true_false.fact: A grassroots football club in the Lake District plays its home games on a pitch with a 1-in-12 slope at one end — the steepest legal gradient for organised football.
true_false.fiction: A 5-a-side league in Slough has banned all 'celebration sliding' after three players in one season tore their shorts and demanded refunds.

true_false.fact: A small Welsh railway station — Llanfair­pwllgwyngyll­gogery­chwyrn­drobwll­llanty­silio­gogogoch — sells more platform-ticket souvenirs than any other UK station.
true_false.fiction: A Norfolk B&B has started offering a 'no chit-chat' room for guests who want to skip breakfast small talk, marked with a polite door card and a £10 surcharge.

true_false.fact: The UK's bestselling biscuit in 2025 wasn't the Custard Cream or the Hobnob — it was the humble digestive, selling over 70 million packs.
true_false.fiction: A Bristol bakery has started selling 'controversial' jam doughnuts injected with mustard instead of jam, claiming a 'small but loyal' customer base.

facts: A cloud can weigh more than a million pounds — about the same as 100 elephants. They float because they're spread across miles of sky.
Honey never spoils. Edible honey has been found in 3,000-year-old Egyptian tombs.
Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood. Two pump blood through the gills, the third pumps it round the body.
The Eiffel Tower can grow up to 15cm taller in summer because the iron expands in the heat.
The dot above a lowercase 'i' or 'j' has a name — it's called a tittle.
Bananas are technically berries. Strawberries, somehow, are not.
Wombats produce cube-shaped poo. Scientists worked out it's the elasticity of their intestines.
A 'jiffy' is an actual unit of time — 1/100th of a second. So 'back in a jiffy' is the fastest promise you can make.
Sharks existed before trees. Sharks have been around for 400 million years; trees only 350 million.
Bubble wrap was originally invented as wallpaper. It flopped, so they pivoted to packaging.
There are more stars in the universe than grains of sand on every beach on Earth.
A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance.
The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896 — it lasted 38 minutes.
Your stomach gets a new lining every three to four days. Otherwise it would digest itself.
The first message ever sent on the internet was 'LO' — they were trying to type 'LOGIN' but the system crashed.
An ostrich's eye is bigger than its brain.
Cows have best friends and get stressed when separated from them.
The word 'OK' began as a misspelling joke in a Boston newspaper in 1839 ('oll korrect').
Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins — up to 40 minutes underwater.
If you could fold a piece of paper 42 times, it would reach the moon.
Bees can recognise human faces.
Saturn would float in water — if you could find a bathtub big enough.
There's a species of jellyfish that is technically immortal — Turritopsis dohrnii.
Antarctica is the only continent without reptiles or snakes.
A blue whale's tongue alone weighs as much as an elephant.
Octopuses can taste with their suckers.
Wales has more castles per square mile than any other country in the world.
Bananas grow upside down.
Your nose can detect over a trillion different scents.
The unicorn is the national animal of Scotland.
There are more trees on Earth than stars in the Milky Way.
A snail can sleep for three years at a time.
The Great Wall of China isn't actually visible from space without aid, despite the myth.
There are more possible games of chess than atoms in the observable universe.
A flock of crows is called a 'murder'.
The unicorn is mentioned in the Bible nine times.
Coca-Cola would be green if colouring wasn't added to it.
A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus.
Polar bears have black skin under their white fur.
Hot water freezes faster than cold water under certain conditions — it's called the Mpemba effect.
The plastic bits on the ends of shoelaces are called aglets.
Penguins propose to their mates by giving them a pebble.
Strawberries aren't actually berries, but bananas, watermelons and pumpkins are.
Cats have a special collarbone that lets them always land on their feet — usually.
The word 'set' has the most definitions of any word in the English language — 430.
The longest English word without a vowel is 'rhythms'.
Ketchup was sold in the 1830s as medicine.
Hippos secrete their own natural sunscreen — a reddish, oily substance.
The colour orange is named after the fruit, not the other way around.
More people speak English in China than in the United States.
Cleopatra lived closer in time to the moon landing than to the building of the Great Pyramid.
A 'jiffy' is an actual unit of time — 1/100th of a second.
Goats have rectangular pupils, which gives them nearly 360-degree vision.
Lightning strikes Earth about 100 times every second.
Bees have to visit roughly 2 million flowers to make one pound of honey.
Mosquitoes are attracted to people who've just eaten bananas.

Masters of the Universe; Power Ballad
Scary Movie 6; new Spielberg film (+ IMAX)
Supergirl: Woman of Tomorrow (+ IMAX); A Private Life
Christopher Nolan's The Odyssey (+ IMAX); Cut Off

Flowervale Street; PAW Patrol 3: The Dino Movie
